content.text: User feedback is the highest-signal data source for evaluating an LLM in production. Unlike offline benchmarks, it reflects actual user satisfaction with real queries in real contexts. Thumb reactions, star ratings, regeneration clicks, and free-text reports form a continuous evaluation loop that can detect quality regressions before they surface in aggregate error rates, seed fine-tuning datasets with hard real-world examples, and prioritise the model improvements that most affect user outcomes.

attrs.alt: User feedback pipeline: capture UI signals, enrich with context, store and tag, close loop into eval and retraining

content.text: Not all feedback signals carry equal information. Explicit signals (thumbs, ratings, reports) are directly labelled; implicit signals (regenerate, copy, abandon) require inference:
Medium — implies prompt was not right

Implicit signals like regenerate are noisier than explicit thumbs, but they are orders of magnitude more frequent. Weight them differently: regenerate might count as 0.3 of a thumbs-down equivalent when aggregating negative rate.
Every feedback event should capture the signal alongside enough context to reproduce the interaction and join it to model version, latency, and retrieval context:

CREATE TABLE llm_feedback (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    session_id  TEXT NOT NULL,
    turn_id     TEXT NOT NULL,
    signal      TEXT NOT NULL,          -- 'thumbs_up' | 'thumbs_down' | 'regenerate' | 'copy' | 'report'
    rating      SMALLINT,               -- 1-5 for star ratings, NULL otherwise
    comment     TEXT,                   -- free-text from 'Report issue'
    model_id    TEXT NOT NULL,
    prompt_hash TEXT NOT NULL,          -- SHA-256 of system_prompt+user_message
    response_id TEXT NOT NULL,
    ttft_ms     INTEGER,
    total_tokens INTEGER,
    label       TEXT,                   -- 'safety' | 'quality' | 'factual' | 'style' (auto-tagged)
    reviewed_at TIMESTAMPTZ,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now()
);

-- Index for time-series dashboard queries
CREATE INDEX ON llm_feedback (created_at, signal);
CREATE INDEX ON llm_feedback (model_id, created_at);

field links feedback to the exact system prompt version in use at the time. When the system prompt changes, feedback from before and after the change should be compared separately to isolate the effect of the change.
Accept feedback from the frontend as a lightweight event and enrich it server-side with context the client does not have access to:

# FastAPI endpoint for frontend feedback events
from fastapi import APIRouter, BackgroundTasks
from pydantic import BaseModel
import hashlib, time

router = APIRouter()

class FeedbackEvent(BaseModel):
    session_id: str
    turn_id: str
    signal: str          # 'thumbs_up' | 'thumbs_down' | 'regenerate' | 'copy' | 'report'
    rating: int | None = None
    comment: str | None = None
    response_id: str

@router.post("/feedback")
async def submit_feedback(event: FeedbackEvent, bg: BackgroundTasks):
    # Attach server-side context immediately — never trust client for these
    record = {
        **event.model_dump(),
        "model_id": get_model_for_session(event.session_id),
        "prompt_hash": get_prompt_hash(event.response_id),
        "ttft_ms": get_latency(event.response_id),
        "total_tokens": get_token_count(event.response_id),
        "created_at": time.time(),
    }
    # Write async — don't block the user's response
    bg.add_task(persist_and_tag, record)
    return {"status": "ok"}

Never trust the client to report model version, token counts, or latency. These must be looked up server-side from the response metadata at capture time. Fire the persist-and-tag operation as a background task to keep the feedback POST endpoint under 10 ms.
Raw feedback signals do not include a defect category. Use an LLM-as-judge to classify negatives into actionable categories: safety, factual error, style, unhelpful, hallucination:

import openai

CATEGORY_PROMPT = (
    'Classify this LLM interaction into exactly one category.\n'
    'Categories: safety, factual_error, style, unhelpful, hallucination, other\n'
    'Respond with JSON: {"category": "...", "confidence": 0.0-1.0}\n\n'
    'User message: {user_message}\n'
    'Model response: {response}\n'
    'User signal: {signal}\n'
    'User comment: {comment}'
)

async def auto_tag(record: dict) -> str:
    # Only tag negatives and reports — thumbs_up is noise for labelling
    if record["signal"] not in ("thumbs_down", "report", "regenerate"):
        return "positive"

    user_msg, response = get_turn_text(record["response_id"])
    prompt = CATEGORY_PROMPT.format(
        user_message=user_msg[:800], response=response[:800],
        signal=record["signal"], comment=record.get("comment", "")
    )
    resp = await openai.AsyncOpenAI().chat.completions.create(
        model="gpt-4o-mini",
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
        response_format={"type": "json_object"},
        max_tokens=64,
    )
    result = json.loads(resp.choices[0].message.content)
    return result.get("category", "other")
Run auto-tagging only on negatives and reports — thumbs-up feedback contains little classification signal. Use a small, fast model (
) to keep tagging costs below $1 per 10 000 feedback events. Log confidence scores; reviews flagged below 0.7 confidence should enter the human review queue.
Human review is expensive. Use reservoir sampling stratified by model version and defect category to maintain a representative review queue without overwhelming annotators:

import random
from collections import defaultdict

# Stratified reservoir sampling: maintain N samples per stratum
STRATUM_BUDGET = 200          # samples per (model_id, category) pair
reservoir: dict[tuple, list] = defaultdict(list)

def stratified_sample(record: dict) -> bool:
    """Returns True if this record is selected for human review."""
    stratum = (record["model_id"], record.get("label", "other"))
    pool = reservoir[stratum]

    # Always include safety-flagged records
    if record.get("label") == "safety":
        pool.append(record)
        return True

    # Reservoir sampling (Algorithm R)
    n = len(pool)
    if n < STRATUM_BUDGET:
        pool.append(record)
        return True
    idx = random.randint(0, n)
    if idx < STRATUM_BUDGET:
        pool[idx] = record
        return True
    return False

Always include 100% of safety-flagged events in the human queue regardless of sample budget. Reserving extra capacity for rare categories (e.g., factual errors) prevents their signal from being washed out by high-volume style complaints.

Negatives that survive human review become regression test cases. Store them in your eval set with the expected behaviour (the human reviewer's preferred response or a rubric score). A growing eval set built from real failures is far more predictive than static benchmark datasets.
When a user clicks Regenerate and then thumbs-up the new response, you have a natural DPO preference pair: the rejected first response and the chosen second response for the same prompt:
These pairs are gold-standard training signal because the preference is revealed by real user behaviour, not a labeller's guess. Accumulate a few thousand high-quality pairs before a fine-tuning run.
Negative Rate as a Rollback Signal
The most actionable time-series metric from feedback is the rolling negative rate per model version:

-- Negative rate rolling 24h per model version
SELECT
    model_id,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE signal = 'thumbs_down') AS negatives,
    COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE signal IN ('thumbs_up', 'thumbs_down')) AS total_rated,
    ROUND(
        100.0 * COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE signal = 'thumbs_down')
        / NULLIF(COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE signal IN ('thumbs_up', 'thumbs_down')), 0),
        2
    ) AS negative_pct
FROM llm_feedback
WHERE created_at > now() - interval '24 hours'
GROUP BY model_id
ORDER BY negative_pct DESC;

Alert when negative rate exceeds 8% in a rolling 24-hour window for any model version. A sudden spike following a model deployment is a strong rollback signal — stronger than latency or error rate, which may be unchanged even when quality degraded.
Hash user IDs and session IDs before writing to the feedback table. Never store raw PII even if the user's comment contains it — strip PII with Presidio before persisting free-text fields.

Keep raw feedback for 90 days for operational review. Archive labelled training examples separately with appropriate consent documentation. GDPR right-to-erasure requests must cascade to feedback records linked to a user.
In many jurisdictions, using feedback to fine-tune the model requires explicit user consent separate from the product's terms of service. Consult legal before using feedback as training data.

Feedback events include prompt hashes, not the raw prompt. Reconstruct the original only when needed for human review, never in automated pipelines, to minimise exposure of potentially sensitive user inputs.
Track feedback events as labelled counters and maintain a rolling negative rate gauge updated by a background job:

from prometheus_client import Counter, Gauge, Histogram

FEEDBACK_EVENTS = Counter(
    "llm_feedback_total",
    "Feedback events received",
    ["signal", "model_id"],
)

NEGATIVE_RATE = Gauge(
    "llm_feedback_negative_rate",
    "Rolling 24h negative rate per model",
    ["model_id"],
)

FEEDBACK_PROCESSING_LATENCY = Histogram(
    "llm_feedback_processing_seconds",
    "Time to persist and tag a feedback event",
    buckets=[0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 5],
)

# Increment on every event submission
# FEEDBACK_EVENTS.labels(signal=event.signal, model_id=model_id).inc()
Three alert tiers: negative rate spike (quality regression), feedback rate drop (instrumentation failure), and safety feedback spike (safety regression):

groups:
  - name: llm_feedback
    rules:
      - alert: FeedbackNegativeRateSpiking
        expr: |
          llm_feedback_negative_rate > 0.08
        for: 30m
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Negative feedback rate > 8% for 30 minutes on {{ $labels.model_id }}"

      - alert: FeedbackRateDropped
        expr: |
          rate(llm_feedback_total[1h]) < 0.001
        for: 2h
        labels:
          severity: warning
        annotations:
          summary: "Feedback signal lost — check frontend instrumentation"

      - alert: SafetyFeedbackSpike
        expr: |
          increase(llm_feedback_total{signal="report"}[30m]) > 20
        for: 0m
        labels:
          severity: critical
        annotations:
          summary: "Spike in user-reported issues — possible safety regression"
alert fires when the feedback event rate falls below background noise level. A frontend deployment that breaks the
endpoint is silent without this check — you would stop seeing thumbs data and not know why.

Feedback data has two endemic biases. First, only engaged users provide feedback — users who abandon early never leave a thumbs-down. Second, users are more likely to report problems than successes, inflating the apparent negative rate. Correct for both:

# Survivorship bias: users who abandon never provide feedback.
# Correct for it by counting abandons as implicit negatives.

import pandas as pd

def compute_adjusted_satisfaction(df: pd.DataFrame) -> pd.Series:
    """
    Adjusted satisfaction = thumbs_up / (thumbs_up + thumbs_down + alpha * abandons)
    alpha = discount factor for abandons (0.3 works empirically for most chatbots)
    """
    alpha = 0.30
    positives = df["signal"].eq("thumbs_up").sum()
    negatives = df["signal"].eq("thumbs_down").sum()
    abandons = df["signal"].eq("abandon").sum()

    denominator = positives + negatives + alpha * abandons
    if denominator == 0:
        return None
    return round(positives / denominator, 4)

# Compare raw vs adjusted satisfaction per model version
# to detect whether a new model merely encourages users to not bother
# clicking thumbs-down rather than genuinely improving quality.

Apply the adjusted satisfaction metric to model comparison dashboards. If a new model shows a lower raw negative rate but a higher adjusted satisfaction, it is genuinely better. If adjusted satisfaction also drops, the lower negative rate may simply reflect reduced engagement.
Do not declare a model 'better' from feedback rates without a significance test. The two-proportion z-test is appropriate for comparing binary negative rates:

# A/B test significance: is model B's negative rate truly lower than A's?
from scipy import stats

def ab_test_negative_rate(
    n_a: int, neg_a: int,
    n_b: int, neg_b: int,
    alpha: float = 0.05,
) -> dict:
    """Two-proportion z-test for negative feedback rates."""
    p_a = neg_a / n_a
    p_b = neg_b / n_b
    p_pool = (neg_a + neg_b) / (n_a + n_b)
    se = (p_pool * (1 - p_pool) * (1/n_a + 1/n_b)) ** 0.5
    if se == 0:
        return {"significant": False, "error": "zero SE"}
    z = (p_b - p_a) / se
    p_value = stats.norm.sf(abs(z)) * 2  # two-tailed
    return {
        "p_a": round(p_a, 4), "p_b": round(p_b, 4),
        "z": round(z, 3), "p_value": round(p_value, 4),
        "significant": p_value < alpha,
        "winner": "B" if (p_value < alpha and p_b < p_a) else ("A" if p_value < alpha else "inconclusive"),
    }

# Example: 5000 turns each, model A has 6% negative, model B has 4.5%
# result = ab_test_negative_rate(5000, 300, 5000, 225)
# => {'p_a': 0.06, 'p_b': 0.045, 'z': 2.99, 'p_value': 0.0028, 'significant': True, 'winner': 'B'}

Rule of thumb: at 5% baseline negative rate and 80% power to detect a 20% relative improvement (from 5% to 4%), you need ~9 000 rated turns per variant. Plan traffic allocation accordingly when configuring A/B experiment buckets.
Only tracking thumbs misses 90% of feedback signal volume. Instrument regenerate, copy, and abandon from day one.
Always filter and compare feedback by model version. Mixing versions dilutes the signal and makes regressions undetectable.
If fewer than 2% of turns receive explicit feedback, prompt the user at conversation end with a lightweight star-rating widget to increase coverage.
MD5 hashing of long prompts has negligible collision risk but truncate to the first 2048 characters to avoid storing very long prompt bodies in the feedback table.

Users often do not detect hallucinations. A high thumbs-up rate on factually incorrect responses is possible. Combine feedback signals with automated factual checks rather than relying solely on user ratings for factual quality.

User feedback captures the gap between offline evaluation and real user satisfaction. Instrument explicit signals (thumb reactions, ratings, reports) and implicit signals (regenerate, copy, abandon) through a lightweight background endpoint that enriches each event with server-side context. Auto-tag negatives with an LLM judge, sample for human review using stratified reservoir sampling, and close the loop by refreshing your eval set with confirmed failures and seeding DPO training with regenerate/thumbs-up preference pairs. Track rolling negative rate per model version as your primary quality health signal in production.
